perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means any information disclosed (whether before or after the date of this Agr eement, in writing, verbally or otherwise and whether directly or indirectly) by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party or an Affiliate of the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Pa rty in connection with the Purpose
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information means only tangible information disclosed after the date of this Agreement, in writing, by the Disclosing Party directly to the Receiving Party in hard copy form related to the Purpose.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is restricted to only 'tangible information disclosed after the date of this agreement', which is narrower than the usual understanding and legal interpretations of confidential information. It excludes verbal disclosures, information disclosed before the agreement, and information provided indirectly or by affiliates. This could be seen as an unreasonable restriction of the scope of protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The definition of trade secret/confidential information under the Economic Espionage Act includes a wide array of information, including intangible information, and does not generally exclude information based solely on the method or timing of disclosure. This modification unduly restricts the definition, potentially conflicting with federal protection of trade secrets.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly restricts the definition of 'Confidential Information' compared to the original text and potentially compared to the standard legal understanding. The original definition included information disclosed 'in writing, verbally or otherwise' and 'directly or indirectly' by affiliates, while the modified version limits it to 'tangible information disclosed after the date of this Agreement, in writing, by the Disclosing Party directly to the Receiving Party in hard copy form'. The Law Explanation states that the Economic Espionage Act includes a wide array of information, including intangible information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1839) concerns the definition of trade secrets, which suggests that federal law protects a broad scope of information. While the snippet doesn't explicitly define 'trade secret' it sets the stage for a more inclusive definition than what is provided in the changed text. The narrowed scope in the modified contract could therefore contradict the broader protections intended by federal law.

perturbation.original_text: Maintaining confidentiality Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Maintaining confidentiality Each party shall, in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party, exercise minimal security measures and a low degree of care, regardless of the measures applied to its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification states that only 'minimal security measures' are required, regardless of the value or sensitivity of the Confidential Information or how the receiving party protects its own confidential information. This can expose confidential information to greater risk and potentially violate implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA requires efforts that are 'reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain secrecy. Prescribing only 'minimal security measures' could be deemed unreasonable, potentially nullifying trade secret protection under state law.

perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly reduces the standard of care for confidential information to 'minimal security measures and a low degree of care,' regardless of the party's own practices. The Law Explanation states that the UTSA requires efforts that are 'reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain secrecy, and prescribing only 'minimal security measures' could be deemed unreasonable. Because the altered contract language dictates minimal security irrespective of circumstances, it directly contradicts what would be considered a reasonable effort to maintain secrecy, which could nullify trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party undertakes within thirty business days of the receipt of a verbal request of the other party or six months after termination of this Agreement, at the option of the receiving party: (a) to attempt to return to the other party some of the other party's Confidential Information and some parts of some documents and other material in its possession; or (b) to consider destroying some of the other party's Confidential Information and some parts of some documents and other material in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: The modification significantly weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. It changes 'three business days' to 'thirty business days', requires only a 'verbal request', delays the post-termination obligation, gives the option to the 'receiving' party instead of the 'disclosing' party, and changes the language to 'attempt to return some' and 'consider destroying some', making the obligation less definitive. This could undermine the entire purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly related to the return of confidential information, failure to adequately protect or destroy copyrighted material embodied within confidential information could potentially run afoul of the DMCA's provisions protecting copyrighted works from unauthorized access or distribution. The weakened return/destruction clauses increase the risk of such violations.
perturbation.location: Section 8 Return or destruction of confidential information

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: DMCA | Electronic Frontier Foundation Skip to main content About Contact Press People Opportunities EFF's 35th Anniversary Issues Free Speech Privacy Creativity and Innovation Transparency International Security Our Work Deeplinks Blog Press Releases Events Legal Cases Whitepapers Podcast Annual Reports Take Action Action Center Electronic Frontier Alliance Volunteer Tools Privacy Badger Surveillance Self-Defense Certbot Atlas of Surveillance Cover Your Tracks Street Level Surveillance apkeep Donate Donate to EFF Giving Societies Shop Sponsorships Other Ways to Give Membership FAQ Donate Donate to EFF Shop Other Ways to Give Search form Search Email updates on news, actions, and events in your area. Join EFF Lists Copyright (CC BY) Trademark Privacy Policy Thanks Electronic Frontier Foundation Donate DMCA DMCA Digital Millennium Copyright Act The Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) contains two main sections that have been a source of particular controversy since they went into effect in 2000. The "anti-circumvention" provisions (sections 1201 et seq. of the Copyright Act) bar circumvention of access controls and technical protection measures. The "safe harbor" provisions (section 512) protect service providers who meet certain conditions from monetary damages for the infringing activities of their users and other third parties on the net. Congress ostensibly passed the "anti-circumvention" provisions of the DMCA to discourage copyright "pirates" from defeating DRM and other content access or copy restrictions on copyrighted works, and to ban the "black box" devices intended for that purpose. In practice the DMCA anti-circumvention provisions have done little to stop "Internet piracy." Yet the DMCA has become a serious threat that jeopardizes fair use, impedes competition and innovation, and chills free expression and scientific research. If you circumvent DRM locks for noninfringing fair uses or create the tools to do so you might be on the receiving end of a lawsuit. The DMCA “safe harbors” protect service providers from monetary liability based on the allegedly infringing activities of third parties. To receive these protections service providers must comply with the conditions set forth in Section 512, including “notice and takedown” procedures that give copyright holders a quick and easy way to disable access to allegedly infringing content. Section 512 also contains provisions allowing users to challenge improper takedowns. Without these protections, the risk of potential copyright liability would prevent many online intermediaries from providing services such as hosting and transmitting user-generated content. Thus the safe harbors, while imperfect, have been essential to the growth of the Internet as an engine for innovation and free expression. EFF has fought hard against the DMCA circumvention provisions in the courts, Congress and other forums, and has fought equally hard to make sure the DMCA safe harbors shelter innovation and creativity. Learn more through the links below. Protect digital p
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the original obligation to protect confidential information. By changing 'three business days' to 'thirty business days', requiring a 'verbal request' instead of a written one, delaying the post-termination obligation, and changing the language to 'attempt to return some' and 'consider destroying some', the modified contract fails to adequately protect confidential information. While the provided law snippets do not directly address these specific changes, the Perturbation Explanation makes it clear that the new language makes the obligation less definitive, undermining the entire purpose of the NDA. This creates a situation where the protection of confidential information is severely compromised.

perturbation.original_text: Any claim for lo sses under this Agreement sha ll be restricted to direct loss only and shall not extend to indirect or cons equential loss.
perturbation.changed_text: Any claim for losses under this Agreement shall be restricted to losses of physical property only and shall not extend to any other type of loss.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting losses to 'physical property only', the contract excludes claims for data breaches and the loss of confidential information. This directly goes against the very spirit of a mutual nondisclosure agreement, as the greatest possible loss is compromise to confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: In case of a computer data breach by a third party because of improper security from the parties, this clause would attempt to limit damages to only physical property, and not the actual data breach. This conflicts with the potential for damages relating to losses from computer fraud.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text limits losses to 'physical property only,' excluding claims for data breaches and loss of confidential information. The Perturbation explanation explicitly states that this change goes against the spirit of a mutual nondisclosure agreement, where the greatest loss is the compromise of confidential information. While the scraped law snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation suggests that this limitation conflicts with potential damages related to losses from computer fraud. The original contract allowed claims for direct losses, but the changed text reduces the scope of what is considered 'loss' in a way that fundamentally undermines the purpose of the agreement, and this would likely fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement sh all be governed by the laws of England and Wales and the part ies hereby submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: The construction, validity and performance of this Agreement shall be governed by the laws of North Korea and the parties hereby submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law and jurisdiction to North Korea makes the contract practically unenforceable for parties located in England and Wales. This effectively nullifies the contract's legal standing for any action taken in the UK, creating a conflict of laws issue if any party tries to enforce the agreement in a different jurisdiction. Also, North Korea does not have much of a court system at all.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UNIDROIT principles are not law, they represent widely accepted principles of contract law, including the concepts of good faith and reasonableness. Selecting a jurisdiction with a fundamentally different legal system and questionable enforcement mechanisms raises concerns about fairness and the parties' ability to obtain a just resolution of disputes, potentially violating these underlying principles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Principle IV.6.9 - Duty to notify / to cooperate | Trans-Lex.org This page uses so called "cookies" to improve its service (i.e. "tracking"). Learn more and opt out of tracking I agree Principles 1672 Bibliography 1035 Materials 86 Archive 66 History & Modern Evolution of Transnational Commercial Law Prof. Dr. Klaus Peter Berger, LL.M. transnational law transnational law (lex mercatoria or international business law) and "Duty to notify / to cooperate" 2020-05-25 13:38:56 https://www.trans-lex.org/img/logo_ball.png Document-Id: 936000, Please cite as: "https://www.trans-lex.org/936000" Principle No. IV.6.9 - Duty to notify / to cooperate (a) Each party is under a good faith obligation to notify in a timely fashion the other party of any problems that occur in the performance of the contract and of any other facts or circumstances on whose knowledge the other party is discernibly dependent, provided that such information can reasonably be expected from that party. (b) Each party is under a good faith obligation to cooperate with the other party when such cooperation can reasonably be expected for the performance of that party's obligations. Commentary References Contract Clauses Commentary 1 This Principle is a direct consequence of the general Principle of good faith . The parties must accept that a contract is not merely a meeting point of conflicting interests, but also, to a certain and varying extent, a common project in which each party must cooperate with the other side. The intensity of the duty to notify and, in particular, the duty to cooperate, depends on the nature of the contract, the professional sophistication of the parties and, in particular, on whether one is dealing with a long-term or one-off contract. 2 The goal of the duty to cooperate in international contracts is to promote and advance contractual performance, to show sincere efforts to further the contractual relationship and to achieve its goals and to refrain from any conduct that would obstruct the contract's implementation, even if these actions are contrary to a party's immediate short term interests. This duty is not without limits. It exists only within the confines of the legitimate expectations of the parties and the standard of reasonableness . Please cite as: "Commentary to Trans-Lex Principle , https://www.trans-lex.org/936000 " References Jump to: Please select a chapter! Nr. 480,4), Paris 1993, at 137 et seq. Blessing, Marc, Das neue internationale Schiedsgerichtsrecht der Schweiz - Ein Fortschritt oder ein Rückschritt?, in: Böckstiegel (ed.), Die internationale Schiedsgerichtsbarkeit in der Schweiz (II), Cologne, Berlin, Bonn, Munich 1989, at 13 et seq. perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies England and Wales as the governing law and jurisdiction, whereas the changed contract designates North Korea. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate principles of good faith and reasonableness, as North Korea has a fundamentally different legal system and questionable enforcement mechanisms. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to Principle IV.6.9 which outlines a 'good faith obligation to cooperate.' Changing the jurisdiction to North Korea makes cooperation and enforcement practically impossible for parties in England and Wales, creating a direct contradiction by undermining the ability to resolve disputes fairly and reasonably, and making contractual performance extremely difficult.
